chapter_summary: Chapter 7 is a poem and a story told by a wise teacher. The teacher looks out of their window and watches a young person make a terrible mistake: instead of holding tight to wisdom, they wander off toward danger and follow someone who leads them completely the wrong way. The chapter is a warning and an invitation: hold on to wisdom like you hold on to your most treasured friend, because wisdom will protect you from paths that look exciting but end in disaster.
main_lesson: God's wisdom is a treasure worth protecting. When we follow wise people and God's Word, we stay safe.

memory_verse.kjv: Keep my commandments, and live; and my law as the apple of thine eye.
memory_verse.little_bible: God says: keep His commandments — they are as precious and important as your own eyes!

parent_guide: Proverbs 7 is an extended narrative poem warning about seduction and the destruction that follows abandoning wisdom. The 'foolish woman' of the chapter represents any temptation that flatters, promises pleasure, and draws someone away from God's path. For children, the entire chapter is adapted around the theme of wisdom as a protector and the danger of ignoring wise counsel. The specific seduction narrative (vv.10-21) is presented as the story of 'a young person who forgot God's wisdom and followed someone who led them the wrong way.' Verses 22-27 draw out the consequences. Parents of older children may read and discuss the fuller meaning of this passage; the children's edition stays age-appropriate throughout.
application_for_children: This week, practise saying: 'Wisdom is my friend.' When you face a hard choice, ask: what would a wise person do? Then do that — even if the other choice looks more exciting.

verses.kjv: My son, keep my words, and lay up my commandments with thee.
verses.little_bible: My child, keep my words close to you — store up God's commandments inside your heart like a treasure.
verses.little_reader_adaptation: Keep God's words close like a treasure.
verses.meaning: Wisdom must be actively stored and guarded. The Hebrew word for 'lay up' pictures carefully stashing something precious where it will not be lost.
verses.memory_phrase: I keep God's words like treasure.
verses.prayer: God, help me keep Your words close — in my heart and in my mind — every day.
verses.discussion_question: What is the most precious thing you own? How do you take care of it so you do not lose it?
verses.family_discussion: How do we store God's Word in our family — in conversation, in memorisation, in daily life?
verses.do_it_today: Write one Bible verse on a piece of paper. Put it in your pocket and read it three times today.
verses.illustration_prompt: A child carefully placing a glowing scroll into a small treasure chest, treating it with great care, warm golden light, watercolour style

verses.kjv: Keep my commandments, and live; and my law as the apple of thine eye.
verses.little_bible: Obey God's commandments and you will truly live! Guard His teaching as carefully as you guard your own eyes.
verses.little_reader_adaptation: Obey God and live! Guard His words like your eyes.
verses.meaning: The 'apple of the eye' is the pupil — the most sensitive, protected part of the body. God's law deserves that level of careful protection.
verses.memory_phrase: Guard God's Word like your own eyes.
verses.prayer: God, help me protect and treasure Your commandments every single day.
verses.discussion_question: Why are your eyes so precious to you? How can you guard God's Word with the same care?
verses.family_discussion: What are practical ways we protect God's Word in our daily lives — in what we watch, say, and choose?
verses.do_it_today: Memorise Proverbs 7:2 as a family today. Say it together three times.
verses.illustration_prompt: A child cupping their hands around a tiny glowing light — protecting it gently and carefully — with God's Word written in golden letters on it, watercolour style

verses.kjv: Bind them upon thy fingers, write them upon the table of thine heart.
verses.little_bible: Tie wisdom on your fingers so you always have it with you — write it on your heart so it is always inside you.
verses.little_reader_adaptation: Write God's wisdom on your heart.
verses.meaning: Wisdom is meant to be embodied — as natural as the hands we see and the heart that beats inside us. True wisdom is not just known; it is lived.
verses.memory_phrase: Write wisdom on your heart.
verses.prayer: God, write Your wisdom so deep into my heart that I never forget it.
verses.discussion_question: What does it mean to have something written on your heart — not just in your head but deep inside you?
verses.family_discussion: How can we help our children not just know wisdom but feel it — have it woven into how they see and respond to the world?
verses.do_it_today: Draw a simple heart on your hand. Each time you notice it today, say a short prayer or a wisdom verse.
verses.illustration_prompt: A child with small golden words written on their open hand and a warm glow on their chest where their heart is, peaceful watercolour scene

verses.kjv: Say unto wisdom, Thou art my sister; and call understanding thy kinswoman:
verses.little_bible: Think of wisdom as your closest friend — your sister! Call understanding your most trusted companion.
verses.little_reader_adaptation: Call wisdom your closest, dearest friend.
verses.meaning: Personifying wisdom as a sister or close relative means making her part of the family — someone you trust, confide in, and turn to naturally.
verses.memory_phrase: Wisdom is my closest friend.
verses.prayer: God, help me treat wisdom like my best friend — always nearby, always trusted.
verses.discussion_question: What does your very best friend do for you? How can wisdom do those same things for you?
verses.family_discussion: What would change in our family if we treated wisdom the way we treat our closest, most trusted relationships?
verses.do_it_today: Before you make any decision today, pause and ask: 'What would wisdom — my best friend — say about this?'
verses.illustration_prompt: A child walking arm in arm with a glowing figure representing wisdom, smiling together like best friends on a sunlit path, warm watercolour style

verses.kjv: That they may keep thee from the strange woman, from the stranger which flattereth with her words.
verses.little_bible: Wisdom and understanding will protect you from people who use sweet, flattering words to lead you the wrong way.
verses.little_reader_adaptation: Wisdom protects you from people with tricky, sweet words.
verses.meaning: The purpose of treasuring wisdom is deeply practical: it guards us when we encounter those who would deceive us through flattery and manipulation.
verses.memory_phrase: Wisdom protects me from tricky words.
verses.prayer: God, let wisdom be my guard against people who say sweet things to lead me wrong.
verses.discussion_question: Have you ever had someone say something very nice to you to get you to do something you should not? How did you know?
verses.family_discussion: How do we build discernment — the ability to tell the difference between genuine kindness and flattery with hidden motives?
verses.do_it_today: Ask a parent to help you practise: they say a compliment, and you decide whether it is true and kind or flattering and tricky.
verses.illustration_prompt: A child standing protected behind a glowing golden shield labelled 'wisdom' as swirling flattering words bounce harmlessly away, watercolour style

verses.kjv: For at the window of my house I looked through my casement,
verses.little_bible: One day, the wise teacher looked out through the window of their house...
verses.little_reader_adaptation: The wise teacher looks out the window.
verses.meaning: The teacher begins a real-life illustration — wisdom is not only taught in words but observed in the stories of those around us.
verses.memory_phrase: Wisdom sees what others miss.
verses.prayer: God, give me eyes that see wisely — help me learn from what I observe around me.
verses.discussion_question: Have you ever watched something happen and learned a lesson from it without being part of it?
verses.family_discussion: How do we help our children learn from observing life — not just from what they are told directly?
verses.do_it_today: Sit quietly for five minutes today and just observe what is happening around you. What do you notice?
verses.illustration_prompt: A kind, wise elder looking thoughtfully out of a warm-lit window at the street below, soft evening light, watercolour style

verses.kjv: And beheld among the simple ones, I discerned among the youths, a young man void of understanding,
verses.little_bible: And in the street below, the teacher noticed a young person — someone who had not yet learned wisdom and was wandering without good direction.
verses.little_reader_adaptation: A young person wanders without wisdom.
verses.meaning: The 'simple' person is not stupid — they are inexperienced and have not yet been shaped by wisdom. This is a warning for the young and for all who drift without wisdom's guidance.
verses.memory_phrase: Without wisdom, we wander.
verses.prayer: God, help me grow in wisdom so I do not wander without direction.
verses.discussion_question: What happens when someone walks around with no plan and no direction? Where do they usually end up?
verses.family_discussion: How do we help our children build wisdom before they face situations that require it?
verses.do_it_today: Think of a wise person in your life — someone who has good direction. Ask them one question about how they make good decisions.
verses.illustration_prompt: A young person wandering aimlessly in a twilight street, looking lost, while a wise elder watches concerned from a window above, watercolour style

verses.kjv: Passing through the street near her corner; and he went the way to her house,
verses.little_bible: The young person walked through the streets and ended up heading toward a place they should not have gone.
verses.little_reader_adaptation: He walked toward the dangerous place.
verses.meaning: Small decisions about direction have big consequences. Getting close to danger is the first step toward it. The young man's path brought him near temptation.
verses.memory_phrase: Stay away from dangerous places.
verses.prayer: God, help me choose the right paths and stay away from places that lead to trouble.
verses.discussion_question: How do little steps in the wrong direction lead to big trouble?
verses.family_discussion: How do we help our children recognise when they are drifting toward a dangerous place — physically, online, or in friendships?
verses.do_it_today: Think about the paths you choose regularly — school, online, friendships. Are they leading you toward wisdom or away from it?
verses.illustration_prompt: A young person's footsteps slowly turning off a bright, well-lit path toward a darker, shadowy street, gentle warning in the image, watercolour style

verses.kjv: In the twilight, in the evening, in the black and dark night:
verses.little_bible: It was twilight — the evening — and the night was becoming dark and full of shadows.
verses.little_reader_adaptation: It was evening — dark and full of shadows.
verses.meaning: The setting is deliberate: darkness represents the absence of wisdom's light. Dangerous choices are often made in 'dark' seasons — when we are not walking in God's light.
verses.memory_phrase: Walk in God's light, not in darkness.
verses.prayer: God, be a light to me in every dark and shadowy moment. Help me not make choices in the dark.
verses.discussion_question: Why do people sometimes make worse choices when they are in the dark — when no one can see, or when they feel far from God?
verses.family_discussion: How does walking close to God and His Word keep us in the light — even when the world around us feels dark?
verses.do_it_today: Read Psalm 119:105 together: 'Your word is a lamp to my feet and a light to my path.' Pray it aloud as a family.
verses.illustration_prompt: A winding street at dusk, growing steadily darker, with one small lantern of golden light far in the distance — a contrast between darkness and God's guiding light, watercolour style

verses.kjv: And, behold, there met him a woman with the attire of an harlot, and subtil of heart.
verses.little_bible: And then someone met him — someone who looked one way on the outside but had very different, hidden plans on the inside.
verses.little_reader_adaptation: Someone met him — looking friendly but planning harm.
verses.meaning: Deception often wears an attractive disguise. True character is hidden in the heart, not visible on the outside — wisdom teaches us to look deeper.
verses.memory_phrase: Not everyone who looks friendly is safe.
verses.prayer: God, give me wisdom to see people's true character — not just how they look or how they act at first.
verses.discussion_question: Have you ever met someone who seemed very nice at first but turned out to be untrustworthy? What helped you see the truth?
verses.family_discussion: How do we teach our children to assess character carefully — looking beyond first impressions?
verses.do_it_today: Think of three things that show a person's true character over time — not in five minutes, but over months.
verses.illustration_prompt: A shadow-faced figure with a bright smile in front and a dark shadow behind — a visual of hidden deception — contrasted with a clear, honest child nearby, gentle watercolour style

verses.kjv: (She is loud and stubborn; her feet abide not in her house:
verses.little_bible: This person was loud, restless, and never stayed settled — always out looking for the next thing.
verses.little_reader_adaptation: She was loud, restless, always looking for trouble.
verses.meaning: Restlessness and a lack of settledness are warning signs. Wisdom cultivates peace and stability; foolishness is always searching for the next thrill.
verses.memory_phrase: Wisdom is calm and settled.
verses.prayer: God, help me be settled and peaceful — not restless and always chasing the next exciting thing.
verses.discussion_question: What is the difference between someone who is joyfully full of energy and someone who is restlessly looking for trouble?
verses.family_discussion: How do we cultivate contentment and peace in our home? How do we help our children be settled rather than restless?
verses.do_it_today: Sit still for five minutes in a quiet place. Ask God to fill you with peace instead of restlessness.
verses.illustration_prompt: A restless figure pacing in shadows, contrasted with a peaceful child sitting in a warm, sunlit room reading — a picture of calm vs. restlessness, watercolour style

verses.kjv: Now is she without, now in the streets, and lieth in wait at every corner.)
verses.little_bible: She was always in the streets, always at the corners, always watching and waiting for the next person to trap.
verses.little_reader_adaptation: She waited at every corner to trap someone.
verses.meaning: Deceptive influences are not passive — they actively seek victims. Wisdom helps us recognise and avoid those who lie in wait to lead us astray.
verses.memory_phrase: Some dangers seek you out — wisdom sees them.
verses.prayer: God, open my eyes to see the traps that are set for me, and help me walk away.
verses.discussion_question: How do you recognise when someone is trying to get you to do something wrong — especially when they are being nice about it?
verses.family_discussion: How do we build the kind of relationship with our children where they feel safe coming to us when they sense a trap or danger?
verses.do_it_today: Tell a trusted adult about one situation you have noticed that felt a little wrong, even if you could not explain why.
verses.illustration_prompt: A figure lurking in the shadows at a street corner waiting, while a wise child notices and walks a safer path with confidence, watercolour style

verses.kjv: So she caught him, and kissed him, and with an impudent face said unto him,
verses.little_bible: She rushed toward him boldly and with great confidence started talking to him as if she had no shame at all.
verses.little_reader_adaptation: She rushed at him boldly with no shame.
verses.meaning: Temptation can be aggressive and shameless. What looks like confidence or boldness can actually be the brazenness of someone with nothing good to offer.
verses.memory_phrase: True boldness comes from wisdom, not from shamelessness.
verses.prayer: God, help me not be impressed by boldness alone. Help me look for wisdom and truth.
verses.discussion_question: Is being bold always a good thing? When is boldness helpful, and when is it dangerous?
verses.family_discussion: How do we teach our children to be confidently kind and truthful — bold for good reasons — rather than bold for selfish ones?
verses.do_it_today: Be bold for something good today — speak up for someone who needs help, or tell the truth when it is easier to stay quiet.
verses.illustration_prompt: A figure rushing forward without shame, contrasted with a child standing firm with calm, wisdom-filled eyes — not afraid but not swayed, watercolour style

verses.kjv: I have peace offerings with me; this day have I payed my vows.
verses.little_bible: She said, 'I have just come from doing religious things — so I must be fine! Come, everything is good.' She used religious words to make wrong things sound right.
verses.little_reader_adaptation: She used religious words to make wrong sound right.
verses.meaning: Religious language and ritual can be misused to create an appearance of goodness while pursuing sin. Wisdom tests what people do, not just what they say.
verses.memory_phrase: Religious words do not always mean a good heart.
verses.prayer: God, help me not just say the right words but truly mean them. Make my heart right, not just my words.
verses.discussion_question: Can someone say all the right religious things and still have a wrong heart? How can you tell?
verses.family_discussion: How do we guard against religion becoming a performance? How do we help our faith stay genuine and true?
verses.do_it_today: Ask yourself honestly: is there any area of your life where you say the right things but do not really mean them? Bring it to God.
verses.illustration_prompt: A figure holding a religious offering with a deceptive smile, the shadow behind them telling the truth their words hide, watercolour illustration

verses.kjv: Therefore came I forth to meet thee, diligently to seek thy face, and I have found thee.
verses.little_bible: 'I was looking for you,' she said. 'I came out just to find you!' She made him feel special — like he was the most important person in the world.
verses.little_reader_adaptation: She made him feel specially chosen to trick him.
verses.meaning: Manipulators often use flattery that makes the target feel uniquely special and sought-out. This tactic lowers our defences and makes us easier to deceive.
verses.memory_phrase: Beware of flattery that makes you feel specially chosen.
verses.prayer: God, remind me that my true worth comes from You — not from what others say to impress me.
verses.discussion_question: How does it feel when someone tells you that you are special? How do you know if they really mean it?
verses.family_discussion: How do we build a child's sense of value in God so they are not easily swayed by manipulation that makes them feel chosen or special?
verses.do_it_today: Tell someone in your family truthfully and specifically what you love about them — not to get anything, just because it is true.
verses.illustration_prompt: A child being told 'I chose you!' by a deceptive figure, looking flattered and uncertain, while a gentle parent in the background radiates genuine love, watercolour style

verses.kjv: I have decked my bed with coverings of tapestry, with carved works, with fine linen of Egypt.
verses.little_bible: She described all sorts of beautiful, luxurious things — making the wrong place sound like the most wonderful place imaginable.
verses.little_reader_adaptation: She made the wrong place sound wonderful.
verses.meaning: Sin is often sold on the packaging — attractive surroundings, beautiful descriptions, appealing imagery. Wisdom looks past the packaging to the destination.
verses.memory_phrase: Beautiful packaging does not make something safe.
verses.prayer: God, help me see past beautiful descriptions to what is really true and good.
verses.discussion_question: Have you ever seen something beautiful that turned out to be not good? A food, a game, or something else?
verses.family_discussion: How do we help our children evaluate choices not just by how they look or sound, but by where they lead?
verses.do_it_today: Before you choose something today, ask: 'Where will this lead me?' — not just 'Does this look good?'
verses.illustration_prompt: A beautifully decorated door that opens onto a dark, dangerous path — the outside looks wonderful but the inside tells the truth, watercolour style

verses.kjv: I have perfumed my bed with myrrh, aloes, and cinnamon.
verses.little_bible: She described wonderful smells and all the pleasures she had prepared — making everything sound irresistible.
verses.little_reader_adaptation: She described pleasures to make it all sound irresistible.
verses.meaning: Temptation appeals to the senses — making the wrong path feel, smell, and taste wonderful in the imagination. Wisdom trains us to evaluate by truth, not by sensation.
verses.memory_phrase: What smells wonderful is not always good.
verses.prayer: God, help me not be led by my senses alone. Teach me to choose by what is true, not just by what feels or sounds amazing.
verses.discussion_question: Can you think of something that smells or looks amazing but is not actually good for you?
verses.family_discussion: How do we train ourselves and our children to choose by values and truth rather than by feelings and sensations alone?
verses.do_it_today: Pick one thing today that is genuinely good for you but not the most exciting option — and choose it anyway.
verses.illustration_prompt: Swirling tempting scents and colours floating attractively through the air, but a child pausing and asking wisdom for help before stepping forward, watercolour style

verses.kjv: Come, let us take our fill of love until the morning: let us solace ourselves with loves.
verses.little_bible: She said, 'Come! Let's just enjoy ourselves all night — forget about tomorrow!' She made wrong choices sound like they were only fun and had no cost.
verses.little_reader_adaptation: She said: forget tomorrow, just have fun now!
verses.meaning: One of sin's most powerful deceptions is the invitation to live only for now — ignoring tomorrow, consequences, and God. Wisdom always considers what comes after.
verses.memory_phrase: Wise people think about tomorrow, not only today.
verses.prayer: God, help me think about tomorrow and not just today. Help me make choices I will be glad about later.
verses.discussion_question: Why do some choices feel amazing right now but turn out to be bad later? Can you think of an example?
verses.family_discussion: How do we help our children develop the habit of thinking about consequences — asking 'what happens next?' before choosing?
verses.do_it_today: Before one choice today, ask: 'Will I still be glad about this tomorrow morning?'
verses.illustration_prompt: A child at a crossroads — one path bright and appealing only for a moment, the other longer but glowing all the way to the horizon — looking thoughtfully forward, watercolour style

verses.kjv: For the goodman is not at home, he is gone a long journey:
verses.little_bible: She said, 'No one will know. No one is watching. No one is home.' She wanted him to think he could do wrong without being seen.
verses.little_reader_adaptation: She said: no one will know, no one is watching.
verses.meaning: Temptation often whispers that no one is watching — no authority, no consequences, no accountability. But God always sees.
verses.memory_phrase: God always sees — even when no one else does.
verses.prayer: God, help me remember that You are always watching — not to catch me, but because You love me and nothing is hidden from You.
verses.discussion_question: Would you behave differently if you knew God was standing right next to you? Why?
verses.family_discussion: How does the truth that God sees everything change how we live — both when someone is watching and when they are not?
verses.do_it_today: Do something good today that only God will see. No one else needs to know.
verses.illustration_prompt: A child who thinks they are unobserved, but a gentle golden light from above shows God's loving, all-seeing presence — not threatening but warm and true, watercolour style

verses.kjv: He hath taken a bag of money with him, and will come home at the day appointed.
verses.little_bible: She kept convincing him: 'It will be fine — we have time, no one will be back for ages.' She made every excuse to push aside wisdom.
verses.little_reader_adaptation: She pushed aside every wise reason to say no.
verses.meaning: When temptation runs out of arguments, it finds new ones — more time, more opportunity, more reasons. Wisdom does not debate — it decides and moves.
verses.memory_phrase: When temptation gives excuses, wisdom decides.
verses.prayer: God, help me not keep listening to excuses. Help me decide quickly for what is right.
verses.discussion_question: Have you ever had someone keep coming up with more and more reasons why you should do something you knew was wrong?
verses.family_discussion: How do we help our children recognise when they are being worn down by excuses and reasoning that keeps pushing them toward wrong?
verses.do_it_today: Practise saying a firm, kind 'no' to something today that is not good for you, even if someone gives you many reasons.
verses.illustration_prompt: A child with hands raised in a gentle but firm 'stop' gesture against a wave of swirling excuses and reasons, standing firm with calm confidence, watercolour style

verses.kjv: With her much fair speech she caused him to yield, with the flattering of her lips she forced him.
verses.little_bible: In the end, all her sweet talk worked. He gave in. He stopped listening to wisdom and followed where she led.
verses.little_reader_adaptation: All her sweet talk finally made him give in.
verses.meaning: Persistent flattery and smooth speech can overwhelm someone who has not kept wisdom close. The antidote is a heart already full of God's truth.
verses.memory_phrase: Sweet talk cannot move a heart full of wisdom.
verses.prayer: God, fill my heart so full of Your truth that sweet, wrong words have no room to take hold.
verses.discussion_question: Why do you think someone might give in to sweet talk even when they know it is wrong?
verses.family_discussion: How do we fill our children's hearts so full of truth and wisdom that flattery and manipulation have less power?
verses.do_it_today: Read one chapter of Proverbs together as a family to fill your hearts with wisdom today.
verses.illustration_prompt: A young person slowly bowing their head and following the deceptive figure, while the light of wisdom stands small but steady behind them — a moment of sad choice, watercolour style

verses.kjv: He goeth after her straightway, as an ox goeth to the slaughter, or as a fool to the correction of the stocks;
verses.little_bible: He walked right into disaster — like an ox that does not know it is walking toward slaughter, or like someone walking into a trap without any idea.
verses.little_reader_adaptation: He walked into disaster without knowing it.
verses.meaning: The tragedy is not cruelty but blindness. The person who abandons wisdom walks cheerfully into destruction, unable to see where the path leads.
verses.memory_phrase: Without wisdom, we walk into traps.
verses.prayer: God, open my eyes so I can see where paths lead before I walk down them.
verses.discussion_question: How can you tell where a path is leading before you have walked all the way down it?
verses.family_discussion: How do we help our children develop the wisdom to evaluate a path's destination — not just its beginning?
verses.do_it_today: Ask a parent or wise adult to look at one path you are on — a friendship, a habit, a hobby — and tell you honestly where they think it leads.
verses.illustration_prompt: A young ox walking peacefully down a road that leads to a shadowed gate — no fear on its face because it does not understand — gentle watercolour, cautionary but not frightening

verses.kjv: Till a dart strike through his liver; as a bird hasteth to the snare, and knoweth not that it is for his life.
verses.little_bible: Like a little bird flying happily into a net — not knowing it is flying toward the end of its freedom — he went, and disaster came.
verses.little_reader_adaptation: Like a bird into a net — he flew into danger.
verses.meaning: The speed and innocence of a bird flying into a snare captures the tragedy of wisdom abandoned. Destruction comes to those who do not see it coming.
verses.memory_phrase: Wisdom helps me see traps before I reach them.
verses.prayer: God, help me see the nets and traps before I fly into them. Give me wisdom to look ahead.
verses.discussion_question: Why do birds fly into nets? What helps animals avoid traps in nature?
verses.family_discussion: What are the 'snares' that our culture sets for young people today, and how do we help our children see them?
verses.do_it_today: Ask a trusted adult: 'What traps do you wish someone had warned you about when you were young?'
verses.illustration_prompt: A small bright bird flying into a net stretched across its path, not yet aware — gentle golden light, a moment of warning frozen in watercolour

verses.kjv: Hearken unto me now therefore, O ye children, and attend to the words of my mouth:
verses.little_bible: So listen, children! Pay attention to what I am telling you — these words are important!
verses.little_reader_adaptation: Listen carefully, children! This matters!
verses.meaning: After the story, the teacher urgently calls for attention. What was illustrated must now be heard and applied by the listener.
verses.memory_phrase: Pay close attention — wisdom is speaking.
verses.prayer: God, help me pay full attention when wisdom speaks — in the Bible, from parents, from teachers.
verses.discussion_question: Why does a teacher sometimes tell a story before they tell you the lesson? What makes a story different from just explaining something?
verses.family_discussion: What stories have shaped your family's values? Which stories do you want your children to carry with them?
verses.do_it_today: Tell your child a story from your own life about a time wisdom saved you — or a time ignoring wisdom cost you something.
verses.illustration_prompt: A wise elder looking directly at a group of children gathered close, leaning in to listen with wide attentive eyes, warm firelit scene, watercolour style

verses.kjv: Let not thine heart decline to her ways, go not astray in her paths.
verses.little_bible: Do not let your heart start drifting toward wrong paths. Do not take even one step down the road that leads to disaster.
verses.little_reader_adaptation: Do not let your heart drift toward wrong paths.
verses.meaning: Moral failure begins with small inclinations of the heart — long before the wrong action. Guard the direction your heart is leaning.
verses.memory_phrase: Guard the direction your heart is leaning.
verses.prayer: God, guard my heart. Do not let it drift toward what is wrong — keep it pointed toward You.
verses.discussion_question: How do you notice when your heart is starting to want something it should not want?
verses.family_discussion: How do we cultivate honest hearts in our home — where family members feel safe saying 'I am starting to want something I should not'?
verses.do_it_today: Ask yourself honestly: is there any direction my heart has been slowly drifting lately? Tell God about it.
verses.illustration_prompt: A compass needle being gently guided back toward true north — a heart reoriented toward wisdom and God — warm steady watercolour illustration

verses.kjv: For she hath cast down many wounded: yea, many strong men have been slain by her.
verses.little_bible: This wrong path has already hurt many, many people — even very strong people who thought they could handle it.
verses.little_reader_adaptation: This wrong path has hurt many — even strong people.
verses.meaning: No one is immune to temptation through their own strength. Many strong, capable people have been destroyed by the path this chapter warns against.
verses.memory_phrase: No one is strong enough to face temptation alone.
verses.prayer: God, help me not be overconfident. I need Your help to stand strong against temptation.
verses.discussion_question: Why do you think even strong and capable people sometimes make terrible mistakes?
verses.family_discussion: How do we cultivate humility about our own weakness — and build accountability and prayer as our real source of strength?
verses.do_it_today: Ask someone you trust to pray with you about an area where you feel tempted or weak.
verses.illustration_prompt: Many sets of footprints leading into the shadows and not coming back — a sober picture of how many have been harmed — gentle watercolour, not frightening but sobering

verses.kjv: Her house is the way to hell, going down to the chambers of death.
verses.little_bible: That wrong path leads all the way down to destruction. But God's path leads all the way up to life!
verses.little_reader_adaptation: The wrong path leads to destruction. God's path leads to life!
verses.meaning: The final warning is stark: the way of folly leads to death, not merely discomfort. This is why wisdom is not optional — it is a matter of life and death.
verses.memory_phrase: God's path leads to life — always choose it.
verses.prayer: God, thank You that Your path leads to life. Help me choose it every single day.
verses.discussion_question: What does it mean that some paths lead to life and some lead to death? Can you think of examples in real life?
verses.family_discussion: How do we help our children see that the choices they make every day are building a path — and that God's path leads somewhere wonderful?
verses.do_it_today: End the day by praying together: 'God, we choose Your path today. Lead us in the way of life.'
verses.illustration_prompt: Two paths diverging: one descending into darkness and shadow, the other rising into golden morning light — a child standing at the fork looking upward toward the light, hopeful watercolour scene
